
# Methods
## Mark-recapture sampling

We released a total of `{r} n_glade_release + n_snakeden_release` adult *Elliptio complanata* at two second-order tributaries to the Potomac River.
We released `{r} n_glade_release` at Glade Run (`{r} coord_glade`) on July 26, 2023, and `{r} n_snakeden_release` at Snakeden Run (`{r} coord_snakeden`) on July 27. 
All releases were uniquely tagged with Hallprint glue-on shellfish tags such that the individual mussel could be later identified. 
Each site was later sampled a total of eight times from July 2023 to September 2024
We sampled the entire area of the site twice by snorkeling (TODO Add table of dates here?). 
For each mussel encountered, we measured its overall length and recorded the unique tag number. 
Any untagged *Elliptio complanata* found on these occasions were tagged. 
Becuase *Elliptio complanata* did not occur at either site prior to the 2023 release, these untagged individuals represented previously tagged mussels whose tags were lost. 

## POPAN description
We were interested primarily in estimating survival of the release cohort over time.
Because we also found a high proportion of dead mussels during sampling, we used Burnham's joint recovery model which incorporates both live encounters and dead recoveries (@BURNHAM 1993) to analyze our mark-recapture data.
The Burnham model is an open population model that allows for the estimation of both apparent survival and population size. 
It has four model parameters that are directly estimated:

1) $S_i$: probability of an individual surviving from occasion $i$ to $i+1$ 
2) $p_i$: probability of an individual being captured alive at occasion $i$ 
3) $r_i$: probability of an individual being recovered dead between occasion $i$ to $i+1$
4) $F_i$: probability of an individual remaining in the sample area (i.e., fidelity)

Because the area for dead and live recoveries was the same, we fixed $F$ to 1 for all occasions. 

## Data Analysis
Release data for the 2023 cohort were combined with data from the eight sampling occasions in 2024 and 2025. 
A set of candidate models were developed under the Burnham model for estimating.
Each candidate model represents a set of hypotheses regarding the four model parameters of the Burnham model (e.g., survival varies over time or is constant).
For all models, $F$ (fidelity) was fixed to 1.
We constructed candidate models where:

1) $S$ varied between each sampling occasion ($t$), was constant between all occasions($.$), or was constant between occasions for the same year but varied between years ($year$),
2) $p$ was constant over all occasions($.$) or was constant between occasions for the same year but varied between years ($year$),
3) $r$ was constant over all occasions($.$) or was constant between occasions for the same year but varied between years ($year$).

This resulted in 12 total models in the candidate model set.
We analyzed each site separately because there was limited connectivity between sites. 

For each site, we analyzed our set of candidate models using Program MARK (@white.burnham_1999_ProgramMARKSurvival) to determine which model had the highest likelihood using an information criteria approach that balances the goodness-of-fit with model complexity to rank the models (@burnham.anderson_2002_ModelSelectionMultimodel).
We used RMARK (an R package that runs MARK) to build and analyze models.
The likelihood of each candidate model was calculated using Akaike’s Information Criterion (AIC) (@akaike_1973_InformationTheoryExtension) modified for small sample sizes (AICc) (@sugiura_1978_FurtherAnalysisData): 
$$𝐴𝐼𝐶𝑐=−2log(𝐿(\hat{θ̂}))+ \frac{2𝐾(𝐾+1)}{𝑛−𝐾−1}$$

where $𝐿(\hat{θ̂})$ is the likelihood of the parameter estimates, given the data, $K$ is the number of parameters, and $n$ is the sample size. 
Normally, the best model is the one with the lowest $AICc$ score and other models are considered competing if $ΔAIC$ <2.0 (@burnham.anderson_2002_ModelSelectionMultimodel). 
Based on this analysis, we selected the preferred model.

Finally, we calculated the expected abundance using survival estimates for the selected model of each site.
The Burnham model does not directly calculate abundance as either a real or derived parameter. 
For this study, we were tracking a single release cohort over time. 
Consequently we calculated the expected abundance using survival estimates from the selected model of each site.
